title: Population ecology
description: Test your knowledge of population ecology!

assessment_items.question_content:
A population of deer in a forest has a maximum per capita growth rate of $0.4$ per year. The population size is limited by the carrying capacity of the forest, which is $900$ individuals.

**Which of the following is the growth rate of the deer population when the population is made up of $300$ individuals?**

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
As the size of the deer population approaches the carrying capacity of the forest, resources will become limiting and the population's growth rate will slow down. We can calculate the growth rate of the deer population in this scenario using the logistic growth equation, shown here:

$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = r_{max}N\left(\dfrac{K - N}{K}\right)$

According to the question text, the maximum per capita growth rate ($r_{max}$) for the deer population is $0.4$ per year, the carrying capacity ($K$) of the deer population’s environment is $900$ individuals, and the number of deer ($N$) in the population at the time point in question is $300$. Plugging these numbers into the equation, we get:

$\begin{align}\dfrac{dN}{dt} &= r_{max}N\left(\dfrac{K - N}{K}\right)\\\\ &= (0.4)(300)\left(\dfrac{900 - 300}{900}\right)\\\\ &= 80\, \text{deer per year}\end{align}$
The growth rate of the deer population when it is made up of $300$ deer is $80$ deer per year.

options.explanation:
$\text{Exponential growth:}$

$\dfrac{dN}{dt} =  r_{max}N$

$\text{Logistic growth:}$

$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = r_{max}N\left(\dfrac{K - N}{K}\right)$

$\text{where:}$

$N = \text{population size}$

$K = \text{carrying capacity}$

$r_{max} = \text{maximum per capita growth rate of population}$

choices.content: $80$ deer per year
$120$ deer per year
$360$ deer per year
$380$ deer per year

assessment_items.question_content:
A population of grey reef sharks in a coral reef has a maximum per capita growth rate of $1.5$ per year. The population size is limited by the carrying capacity of the reef, which is $50$ individuals.

**Which of the following is the growth rate of the shark population when the population is made up of $30$ individuals?**

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
As the size of the shark population approaches the carrying capacity of the coral reef, resources will become limiting and the population's growth rate will slow down. We can calculate the growth rate of the shark population in this scenario using the logistic growth equation, shown here:

$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = r_{max}N\left(\dfrac{K - N}{K}\right)$

According to the question text, the maximum per capita growth rate ($r_{max}$) for the shark population is $1.5$ per year, the carrying capacity ($K$) of the shark population’s environment is  $50$ individuals, and the number of sharks ($N$) in the population at the time point in question is $30$. Plugging these numbers into the equation, we get:

$\begin{align}\dfrac{dN}{dt} &= r_{max}N\left(\dfrac{K - N}{K}\right)\\\\ &= (1.5)(30)\left(\dfrac{50 - 30}{50}\right)\\\\ &= 18\, \text{sharks per year}\end{align}$
The growth rate of the shark population when it is made up of $30$ sharks is $18$ sharks per year.

options.explanation:
$\text{Exponential growth:}$

$\dfrac{dN}{dt} =  r_{max}N$

$\text{Logistic growth:}$

$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = r_{max}N\left(\dfrac{K - N}{K}\right)$

$\text{where:}$

$N = \text{population size}$

$K = \text{carrying capacity}$

$r_{max} = \text{maximum per capita growth rate of population}$

choices.content: $18$ sharks per year
$45$ sharks per year
$48$ sharks per year
$75$ sharks per year

assessment_items.question_content:
A population of three-toed sloths in a tropical forest has a maximum per capita growth rate of $0.8$ per year. The population size is limited by the carrying capacity of the forest, which is $500$ individuals.

**Which of the following is the growth rate of the sloth population when the population is made up of $275$ individuals?**

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
As the size of the sloth population approaches the carrying capacity of the forest, resources will become limiting and the population's growth rate will slow down. We can calculate the growth rate of the sloth population in this scenario using the logistic growth equation, shown here:

$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = r_{max}N\left(\dfrac{K - N}{K}\right)$

According to the question text, the maximum per capita growth rate ($r_{max}$) for the sloth population is $0.8$ per year, the carrying capacity ($K$) of the sloth population’s environment is $500$ individuals, and the number of sloths ($N$) in the population at the time point in question is $275$. Plugging these numbers into the equation, we get:

$\begin{align}\dfrac{dN}{dt} &= r_{max}N\left(\dfrac{K - N}{K}\right)\\ &= (0.8)(275)\left(\dfrac{500 - 275}{500}\right)\\ &= 99\, \text{sloths per year}\end{align}$
The growth rate of the sloth population when it is made up of $275$ sloths is $99$ sloths per year.

options.explanation:
$\text{Exponential growth:}$

$\dfrac{dN}{dt} =  r_{max}N$

$\text{Logistic growth:}$

$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = r_{max}N\left(\dfrac{K - N}{K}\right)$

$\text{where:}$

$N = \text{population size}$

$K = \text{carrying capacity}$

$r_{max} = \text{maximum per capita growth rate of population}$

choices.content: $99$ sloths per year
$220$ sloths per year
$374$ sloths per year
$400$ sloths per year

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of researchers measured the size of a population of plants over time. Their results are shown in the graph below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following parts of the graph best represent when the growth of the plant population was NOT limited by the availability of resources such as water, sunlight, or minerals?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph with Population size on the y axis and Time on the x axis. The graph is broken up into 4 labeled segments and one unlabeled segment. In segment A a line begins at the origin and slowly increases. In segment B the line begins to increase more rapidly. In segment C the line increases very rapidly and then begins to slow. In segment D the line becomes horizontal before slowly decreasing. In the last unlabeled segment the line decreases before beginning to slowly increase again.

choices.content: A and B
B only
C only
C and D

In the last unlabeled segment the line decreases before beginning to slowly increase again.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph below represents the size of a bird population over time.

[[☃ image 1]]

The population experiences a period of heavy rainfall that causes the number of edible seeds in the birds’ habitat to increase.

**Which of the following graphs represents how the size of the bird population would likely be affected over time by this environmental change?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph with Population size on the y axis and Time on the x axis. A curve begins at the origin and increases rapidly until it nears the top of the y axis. The curve then begins to curve down slightly before increasing and then decreasing again.

choices.content: ![A line graph with Population size on the y axis and Time on the x axis. A legend shows that a solid line indicated Before rainfall and a dashed line indicates After rainfall. A solid curve begins at the origin and increases rapidly until it is three-fourths of the way up the y axis. The solid curve then begins to curve down slightly before increasing and then decreasing again. At this point the solid curve becomes a dashed line, which increases rapidly and then continues near the top of the y axis.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/262b0dcc5f6918f09f1dadfbd1145e3b79de3d04)

![A line graph with Population size on the y axis and Time on the x axis. A legend shows that a solid line indicated Before rainfall and a dashed line indicates After rainfall. A solid curve begins at the origin and increases rapidly until it is three-fourths of the way up the y axis. The solid curve then begins to curve down slightly before increasing and then decreasing again. At this point the solid curve becomes a dashed line, which increases briefly before decreasing rapidly to near the bottom of the y axis.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/14aa198d2b392b02d8627b4e20c3cea014ae8458)

![A line graph with Population size on the y axis and Time on the x axis. A legend shows that a solid line indicated Before rainfall and a dashed line indicates After rainfall. A solid curve begins at the origin and increases rapidly until it nears the top of the y axis. The solid curve then begins to curve down slightly before increasing and then decreasing again in an undulating wave. At this point the solid curve becomes a dashed line, which continues near the top of the y axis.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bafc418fed0e1011c92176f04a340fa3cac7c491)

![A line graph with Population size on the y axis and Time on the x axis. A legend shows that a solid line indicated Before rainfall and a dashed line indicates After rainfall. A solid curve begins at the origin and increases rapidly until it nears the top of the y axis. The solid curve then begins to curve down slightly before increasing and then decreasing again in an undulating wave. At this point the solid curve becomes a dashed line, which decreases rapidly until it reaches halfway down the y axis. **Which of the following graphs represents how the size of the panda population would likely be affected over time by this environmental change?**

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of scientists carried out a long-term study of sea urchin population dynamics off the coast of Japan. Over a number of years, the scientists tracked sea urchin abundance and development in the area. During this time, they also recorded all local occurrences of red tides, which are algal blooms that can produce toxins that harm sea life. A subset of the scientists’ data is shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

[[☃ image 3]]

**Which of the following statements is the most likely explanation for the decrease in sea urchin abundance seen between 1980 and 1990?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Ohgaki, Shun-Ichi, Tetsuya Kato, Naomasa Kobayashi, Hidetomo Tanasea, Naoki H. Kumagai, So Ishida, Tomoyuki Nakano, Yoko Wada, and Yoichi Yusa. 2019. “Effects of Temperature and Red Tides on Sea Urchin Abundance and Species Richness Over 45 Years in Southern Japan” *Ecological Indicators* 96:684-93. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolind.2018.03.040.

options.alt: A line graph with H. crassispina abundance on the y axis and Time (year) on the x axis. A solid line begins at (1982, 260) and decreases in a jagged manner until reaching a minimum at (1989, 30). The line increases slowly until (1996, 120) when it rapidly increases to (1997, 280). The line then decreases in a jagged manner until it ends at (2000, 200).
options.caption: **Figure 1.** Yearly counts of *Heliocidaris crassispina* sea urchin abundance

options.alt: A line graph with H. crassispina abnormality on the y axis and Time (year) on the x axis. A solid line begins at (1980, 0.5) and decreases to (1981, 0). The line increases in a jagged manner until (1986, 2.5) before decreasing in a jagged manner until (1994, 0). The line increases until (1996, 1) before decreasing until (2000, 0).
options.caption: **Figure 2.** Levels of abnormality observed in sea urchin embryos grown in water collected from the study site each year, represented using an abnormality index. A higher abnormality index number indicates that a larger proportion of embryos developed abnormally.

options.alt: A line graph with Red tide (days per year) on the y axis and Time (year) on the x axis. A solid line begins at (1980, 20) and increases in a jagged manner until (1983, 80). The line then decreases until (1985, 45), increases until (1987, 90), and decreases in a jagged manner until (1993, 10). The line then increases to (1994, 58) and then decreases in a jagged manner until (1999, 5) and slowly increasing until (2000, 10).
options.caption: **Figure 3.** Number of days per year of red tide

choices.content: The sea urchin population was reduced by a density-independent factor that had a negative effect on embryo development.
The sea urchin population was reduced by a density-dependent factor that had a positive effect on embryo development.
The sea urchin population was reduced by a density-independent factor that directly caused the environment’s carrying capacity for sea urchin embryos to decrease.
The sea urchin population was reduced by a density-dependent factor that directly caused the environment’s carrying capacity for sea urchin embryos to increase.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of scientists studied a population of orcas living in the northeastern Pacific Ocean. This population eats various species of salmon, including Chinook, chum, sockeye, and pink salmon, along with other fish such as lingcod and halibut.

The scientists used demographic data that had been collected over a 25-year period to calculate the orca population’s mortality rate over time (Figure 1). 

[[☃ image 1]]
**Figure 1.** The orca population’s mortality rate from 1979-2003, displayed as a mortality index. A higher mortality index indicates a higher mortality rate. 

The scientists then used data that had been gathered about Chinook salmon in the same geographic location to determine the salmon’s abundance over the same time period (Figure 2).

[[☃ image 2]]
**Figure 2.** Abundance of Chinook salmon in the orca population’s territory, displayed as an abundance index. A higher abundance index indicates a higher abundance of Chinook salmon.

**Which of the following statements is best supported by the data provided above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A bar graph with Orca mortality index on the y axis and Year on the x axis. The orca mortality index increases from 1980 to 1982, decreases from 1982 to 1990, increases from 1990 to 2000, and decreases from 2000 to 2003.

A bar graph with Chinook salmon abundance index on the y axis and Year on the x axis.The Chinook salmon abundance index decreases from 1980 to 1983, increases from 1983 to 1987, decreases from 1987 to 1995, increases from 1995 to 1998, decreases from 1998 to 2000, and increases from 2000 to 2003.

choices.content: Higher mortality rates in the orca population coincided with lower Chinook salmon abundance, indicating that Chinook salmon are a limiting resource for the orca population.
Lower mortality rates in the orca population coincided with lower Chinook salmon abundance, indicating that salmon species other than Chinook are the primary food source for the orca population.
More orcas died in years when Chinook salmon were plentiful, indicating the mortality rate was influenced primarily by a density-independent factor in the orcas’ environment.
Fewer orcas died in years when Chinook salmon were scarce, indicating the mortality rate was primarily influenced by a density-dependent factor in the orcas’ environment.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kelp perch are fish that live in close association with giant kelp, a type of brown algae. Giant kelp grows in tree-like stalks that stretch from the seafloor to the ocean’s surface. The kelp provides the perch with protection from predators, including kelp bass.

A scientist studied kelp bass predation of kelp perch in a reef off the coast of Southern California. To do this, the scientist set up four plots of equal size, each containing four giant kelp stalks. Then, the scientist added different numbers of perch to each plot, and observed how many of the perch were killed by nearby kelp bass over a set time period. 

The results of the study are shown in Figure 1, which shows the proportion of fish from each population that were killed (the proportional mortality) versus the population’s density.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Figure 1.** Relationship between kelp perch proportional mortality and density.

**Which of the following claims is best supported by the data provided above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph with Proportional mortality on the y axis and Kelp perch density (number per plot) on the x axis. A line increases through the following points: (10, 0.2), (20, 0.38), (40, 0.42), and (60, 0.75).

choices.content: Kelp perch population size is controlled by kelp bass predation in a density-dependent manner.
Kelp perch population size is controlled by kelp bass predation in a density-independent manner.
At high perch population densities, there were likely fewer available sheltering sites in the kelp, which led to a density-independent increase in predation.
At low perch population densities, there were likely fewer available sheltering sites in the kelp, which led to a density-dependent increase in predation.
